runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 4 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Chetty (2012) 'Bounds on Elasticities With Optimization Frictions', Econometrica — surveys compensated labor-supply elasticities and notes modest values (~0.33) for the intensive margin
Congressional Budget Office (2012) labor supply model documentation — uses primary-earner substitution elasticities around 0.25 for middle-income groups
Blundell & MaCurdy (1999) 'Labor Supply: A Review of Alternative Approaches' — documents that primary/married-male intensive-margin elasticities cluster between 0.1 and 0.4
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners (typically married men or household heads) in decile 4 sit just below median income. The extensive-margin response for primary earners is generally small; the relevant intensive-margin (substitution) elasticity in mainstream U.S. microsimulation models (e.g., CBO, Tax Policy Center) is commonly set around 0.25 for this demographic. The broader empirical literature (Chetty 2012, Blundell & MaCurdy 1999) places compensated intensive-margin elasticities for primary earners between roughly 0.1 and 0.4, with a central tendency near 0.2–0.3. Decile 4 earners face moderate effective marginal rates and no strong reason to differ substantially from the median; I anchor on 0.25 with moderate uncertainty, allowing values as low as 0.05 (near-zero response) and as high as 0.50 in the tails.

The extensive-margin response for primary earners is generally small; the relevant intensive-margin (substitution) elasticity in mainstream U.S. microsimulation models (e.g., CBO, Tax Policy Center) is commonly set around 0.25 for this demographic. The broader empirical literature (Chetty 2012, Blundell & MaCurdy 1999) places compensated intensive-margin elasticities for primary earners between roughly 0.1 and 0.4, with a central tendency near 0.2\u20130.3. Decile 4 earners face moderate effective marginal rates and no strong reason to differ substantially from the median; I anchor on 0.25 with moderate uncertainty, allowing values as low as 0.05 (near-zero response) and as high as 0.50 in the tails."}

runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 4 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Chetty (2012, Annual Review of Economics) - compensated labor supply elasticities meta-analysis suggesting ~0.33 for extensive/intensive margin combined
CBO (2012) - labor supply elasticity assumptions for primary earners around 0.15–0.3 compensated
Keane (2011, Journal of Economic Literature) - structural estimates of male labor supply elasticities often 0.1–0.4
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners in decile 4 are lower-middle earners, where labor supply responsiveness tends to be modest. The literature on compensated wage elasticities for primary (typically male) earners generally clusters between 0.1 and 0.4, with central estimates around 0.2–0.3. Decile 4 earners face moderate effective marginal tax rates and are not at the extremes where behavioral responses are largest. I center my belief at 0.25, reflecting mainstream U.S. microsimulation calibrations (e.g., CBO-style models) which use compensated elasticities roughly in the 0.15–0.30 range for primary earners, with uncertainty spanning 0.05 to 0.50 across the 90% interval.

runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 4 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Chetty (2012) 'Bounds on Elasticities With Optimization Frictions,' Econometrica — reviews compensated labor supply elasticities for primary earners, generally in the 0.1–0.5 range
Congressional Budget Office (2012) labor supply elasticity assumptions for microsimulation — uses substitution elasticities around 0.25 for primary earners

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners (typically male household heads or higher-earning spouses) are generally found to have relatively inelastic labor supply on the intensive margin. The literature consensus for compensated (substitution) elasticities for primary earners clusters between 0.1 and 0.4, with many microsimulation models (e.g., CBO-style models) adopting values near 0.25. Decile 4 represents a lower-middle income group; there is some evidence that elasticities may be slightly higher in lower deciles due to greater constraint flexibility, but the difference is modest. I center my belief at 0.25 with moderate uncertainty, allowing for values as low as 0.05 (near-zero response) or as high as 0.50 (upper bound commonly used in sensitivity analyses).

runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 4 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Chetty, R. (2012). Bounds on Elasticities with Optimization Frictions. Econometrica.
CBO (2012). Labor Supply and Taxes. Congressional Budget Office.
Saez, E., Slemrod, J., & Giertz, S. (2012). The Elasticity of Taxable Income with Respect to Marginal Tax Rates. Journal of Economic Literature.
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners (typically heads of household) exhibit relatively inelastic labor supply responses compared to secondary earners. For decile 4 (lower-middle income range), workers are generally below the standard deduction phase-in and facing moderate effective marginal tax rates. The literature on compensated (substitution) elasticities for primary earners clusters around 0.1–0.3, with the central tendency near 0.2 for this income range. Microsimulation models (e.g., CBO, Tax Policy Center) typically calibrate primary-earner substitution elasticities between 0.1 and 0.4, with lower-decile workers sometimes assigned slightly lower values due to wage rigidities and full-time constraints. The bounded-change framing (Chetty 2012) may compress estimates toward the center. I center my belief at 0.20 with meaningful uncertainty spanning roughly 0.05 to 0.45.

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners (typically male household heads) are generally found to have modest labor supply elasticities compared to secondary earners. Microsimulation models (e.g., CBO-style) often assign substitution elasticities around 0.25 for primary earners in this income range. I center my belief at 0.25 with moderate uncertainty reflecting variation across studies and model specifications.

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners in the middle of the earnings distribution (decile 4, roughly lower-middle income) tend to exhibit modest compensated (substitution) labor supply elasticities. Uncertainty around this central value reflects variation across studies, model specifications, and sample periods.

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners (typically male household heads) are generally found to have relatively inelastic labor supply, with compensated (substitution) elasticities in the range of 0.1–0.4 in most empirical U.S. studies. Decile 4 represents lower-middle income workers, where effective marginal rates from the tax-benefit system (including phase-outs of EITC and other transfers) can be moderately high, potentially raising behavioral responsiveness slightly above the very bottom deciles but still within the modest range typical for primary earners. Microsimulation models (e.g., those used by CBO or Tax Policy Center) typically parameterize primary-earner substitution elasticities around 0.2–0.3 for middle-to-lower-middle earners. I center my belief at 0.25, with meaningful uncertainty spanning roughly 0.05 to 0.50.

I center my belief at 0.25, with meaningful uncertainty spanning roughly 0.05 to 0.50."}

Do not use tools, files, the web, code, or external resources.
Do not try to reconstruct a literature review or search for a consensus estimate.

- Target interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 4 in a U.S. microsimulation model
- Population/context: Primary earners in earnings decile 4 in the United States under a current-law tax-benefit simulation
